---
title: "Quantiles and Conditional Quantiles"
subtitle: "Moving Beyond the Conditional Mean"
author: "Tatjana Kecojević"
date: "24 June 2026"

format:
  revealjs:
    theme: serif
    slide-number: true
    logo: UoM_logos/UoM_logo_TAB_col_white_background.png
    title-slide: false
editor: visual
---

# Learning Objectives

By the end of this session you will be able to:

- Define quantiles and percentiles
- Distinguish between unconditional and conditional quantiles
- Explain why quantiles provide information beyond averages
- Interpret growth charts as conditional quantiles
- Understand the motivation for quantile regression

---

## Why might average effects be insufficient?

![](figures/policy_comparison.png){width=50%}

Two policies can have similar average effects but very different consequences across the distribution.

---

## Why Go Beyond the Mean?

 Many social science questions focus on:

- Wages
- Health outcomes
- Educational achievement
- Housing prices

Traditional regression focuses on: **The conditional mean**

But important differences may occur across the entire distribution.

---

## A Historical Motivation

One of the earliest attempts to study human variation was undertaken by:

### Adolphe Quetelet (1796–1874)

![](images/Quetelet.png){width="80%"}

---

## Beyond the "Average Person"

Quetelet introduced the idea of the

### *l'homme moyen* (the average man)

However:

- Individuals differ substantially
- Populations exhibit variation
- Understanding distributions is often more informative than understanding averages

---

## Thinking About Percentiles

Suppose a doctor tells a parent: <span style="color:#1f77b4;">*Your child is at the 25th percentile for weight.*</span>

What does this mean?

<span style="color:red;">Percentiles Describe Position</span>

A child at the 25th percentile:

- weighs more than roughly 25% of children
- weighs less than roughly 75% of children

A percentile indicates: Position within a distribution not distance from an average.

---

## What Is a Quantile?

A quantile is a value below which a specified proportion of observations falls.

Examples:

- 25th percentile
- 50th percentile (median)
- 75th percentile

---

## Quantiles Divide a Distribution

![](figures/quantiles_distribution.png)

Quantiles divide an ordered distribution into meaningful sections.

---

## Quartiles

Quartiles divide the distribution into four equal parts.

| Quartile | Percentile    |
|----------|---------------|
| Q1       | 25th          |
| Q2       | 50th (Median) |
| Q3       | 75th          |

---

## The Median

The median is the most familiar quantile.

### 50th percentile

- Half of observations lie below it
- Half lie above it

Unlike the mean, it is robust to extreme values.

---

## Quantiles Summarised in a Boxplot

![](figures/boxplot_quantiles.png)

---

## Reading a Boxplot

The boxplot displays:

- Q1 (25th percentile)
- Median (50th percentile)
- Q3 (75th percentile)

The box contains the middle 50% of observations.

The whiskers and points show the tails of the distribution.

::: {.callout-note}
## Boxplot whiskers and outliers

Whiskers extend to the furthest observations within **1.5 × IQR** of the quartiles. Points beyond these limits are shown individually as **potential outliers**.
:::

---

## Why Quantiles Matter

The boxplot immediately reveals:

- skewness
- spread
- outliers

Information that is invisible from the mean alone.

---

## Unconditional Quantiles

So far we have considered quantiles calculated from:

### The entire sample

Examples:

- Median wage
- 90th percentile of income
- 25th percentile of house prices

These are called: <span style="color:#1f77b4;">Unconditional quantiles</span>

---

## Questions Answered by Unconditional Quantiles

Examples:

- What is the median wage?
- What income separates the richest 10%?
- What house price marks the lowest quartile?

<span style="color:red;">Useful...</span> <span style="color:#1f77b4;">but often not sufficient.</span>

---

## Do All Groups Look the Same?

Consider wage distributions for workers with different educational attainment.

#### Wage Distributions by Education

![](figures/wage_distributions_by_education.png)

---

## What Do We Observe?

Workers with higher education tend to have:

- higher wages overall
- higher upper-tail earnings
- different distributional shapes

The overall distribution hides these differences.

---

## Conditional Quantiles

::: {.callout-tip}
## Key Idea
A **conditional quantile** describes:    
**The distribution of an outcome given specific characteristics.**
:::

Examples:

- Wage given education
- Income given occupation
- Weight given age

---

## Unconditional vs Conditional Quantiles

| <span style="color:#1f77b4;">Unconditional Quantiles</span>     | <span style="color:#1f77b4;">Conditional Quantiles</span>             |
|-----------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| Entire population           | Specific subgroup                 |
| Overall wage distribution   | Wage distribution given education |
| Overall weight distribution | Weight distribution given age     |

---

## A Familiar Example

Most people have already encountered conditional quantiles.

### Growth charts

![](images/OverlapGWtFinal.png)

---

## How Do Growth Charts Work?

Each curve represents a different percentile:

- 5th percentile
- 25th percentile
- 50th percentile
- 75th percentile
- 95th percentile

These percentiles are estimated:

### Conditional on age

---

## Interpreting a Growth Chart

A child on the 25th percentile curve:

- weighs more than approximately 25% of children of the same age
- weighs less than approximately 75% of children of the same age

Age is the conditioning variable.

---

## The Key Insight

::: {.callout-important}

## Key Insight
Growth charts <span style="color:red;">are not</span> models of the **average child**.

They model **conditional quantiles**, describing the entire distribution of weight given age.
:::

Social scientists ask similar questions:

- How does education affect wages?
- How does class size affect achievement?
- How does socio-economic status affect health?

<span style="color:red;">Why focus only on the mean?</span>

---

## OLS vs Quantile Regression

**OLS estimates:** <span style="color:#1f77b4;">Conditional means</span>

**Quantile regression estimates:** <span style="color:#1f77b4;">Conditional quantiles</span> allowing effects to differ across the outcome distribution.


Instead of asking:

> How does education affect average wages?

We can ask:

- How does education affect low earners?
- How does education affect median earners?
- How does education affect top earners?

---

## Take-Home Messages

- Quantiles describe positions within a distribution
- Quartiles and percentiles are quantiles
- Unconditional quantiles summarise the whole population
- Conditional quantiles describe subgroups
- Growth charts are conditional quantile models
- Quantile regression extends this idea to regression analysis

---

## Next Section

### Introducing Quantile Regression

- Conditional quantile functions
- The quantile regression model
- Estimation using the check loss function
- Comparing OLS and quantile regression
